description: An H2-receptor blocker (brand Pepcid) that reduces stomach acid, used for heartburn, reflux, and ulcers. It is largely cleared unchanged by the kidneys.

halfLife.conditions: FDA Pepcid (famotidine) label: 'famotidine has an elimination half-life of 2.5-3.5 hours' in subjects with normal renal function. Point value 3 h; band 2.5-3.5 h. Half-life exceeds 20 h in severe renal impairment (CrCl <10 mL/min); this file models normal renal function.

vd.conditions: the FDA label characterises distribution only qualitatively and prints no numeric volume; the steady-state volume of distribution 1.0-1.3 L/kg is from Echizen & Ishizaki, 'Clinical Pharmacokinetics of Famotidine' (Clin Pharmacokinet 1991), as tabulated in the famotidine StatPearls chapter (accessed this pass). Point value 1.15 L/kg (mid-range) lands the single-dose peak on the reported dose-proportional Cmax (see magnitude check in notes). Famotidine is only 15-20% protein bound. Scaled to absolute litres against the 70 kg reference subject at derivation time.

F.conditions: FDA Pepcid label: 'the bioavailability of oral doses is 40 to 45%'. Point value 0.43. The incomplete, dose-independent absorption means oral Cmax is well below the IV level at equal dose - a contrast to the ~100%-bioavailable metronidazole and the ~50% atenolol.

tmax.conditions: FDA Pepcid label: 'peak famotidine plasma levels occur in 1 to 3 hours'. Representative point value 2 h; ka derived from this Tmax at derivation time.

fda_label.title: PEPCID (famotidine) tablets - FDA Structured Product Label, Clinical Pharmacology / Pharmacokinetics (oral bioavailability 40-45%; peak plasma levels at 1-3 h; elimination half-life 2.5-3.5 h with normal renal function; 15-20% protein bound; eliminated by renal (65-70%) and metabolic (30-35%) routes; the only metabolite identified in humans is the S-oxide; plasma levels after multiple dosages are similar to those after single doses; IV concentration-time profile is biexponential)

famotidine_review.title: Echizen H, Ishizaki T. Clinical Pharmacokinetics of Famotidine. Clin Pharmacokinet 1991 (steady-state volume of distribution 1.0-1.3 L/kg) - value as tabulated in the famotidine StatPearls chapter (NBK534778), accessed this pass

notes:
A clean LINEAR one-compartment histamine H2-receptor antagonist (a new drug class for the seed set), curated from the FDA Pepcid label for disposition and oral parameters, with the volume of distribution from the Echizen & Ishizaki clinical-pharmacokinetics review (the label prints no numeric Vd). Reported PK: oral bioavailability 40-45%, Tmax 1-3 h, elimination half-life 2.5-3.5 h (normal renal function), 15-20% protein binding, and predominantly renal elimination (65-70% excreted unchanged, 30-35% metabolic).

WHY FAMOTIDINE - it adds the H2-antagonist / acid-suppression class and is a textbook clean linear one-compartment renally-cleared drug, reinforcing the renal-elimination thread (atenolol, vancomycin, gentamicin, acyclovir) alongside a distinctly GI indication. Its ~43% oral bioavailability is a useful middle point between the ~100%-absorbed metronidazole and the low-F flip-flop acamprosate: at equal dose the oral Cmax sits well below the IV level, but exposure is still dose-stable because absorption is incomplete-but-constant, not saturable.

LINEARITY. The FDA label states 'plasma levels after multiple dosages are similar to those after single doses' - i.e. no accumulation and dose-proportional kinetics. Elimination is first-order (chiefly renal tubular secretion plus minor CYP-mediated S-oxidation), non-saturable at therapeutic doses, so a single half-life is meaningful and superposition of repeated doses is valid. linear: true.

ONE-COMPARTMENT COLLAPSE. The label notes the IV concentration-time profile 'exhibits a biexponential decay' with a short distribution half-life (~0.18-0.5 h) ahead of the 2.5-3.5 h elimination phase. That distribution phase is shallow and brief, so famotidine is modelled as one compartment (the documented monophasic-collapse posture of metronidazole/cefotaxime/ibuprofen): faithful to the terminal slope and to Cmax, with a slightly idealised early shape.

VOLUME / MAGNITUDE CHECK (ceiling test). For a one-compartment model F.D/V is a hard ceiling on the peak. With Vd 1.15 L/kg (80.5 L for the 70 kg subject), a 40 mg oral dose gives F.D/V = 0.43 x 40 / 80.5 = 0.214 mg/L = 214 ng/mL, comfortably above the reported oral Cmax, so the one-compartment collapse works. Building the engine curve (F = 0.43, ke = ln2/3 h, ka from Tmax 2 h) gives a 40 mg oral peak around 120-130 ng/mL and a 20 mg peak around 65 ng/mL - consistent with reported single-dose Cmax values (a 20 mg oral study reported ~67 ng/mL) scaled dose-proportionally. Vd 1.15 L/kg (mid of the cited 1.0-1.3 L/kg range) is the Cmax-consistent choice.

ACTIVE METABOLITE (not modelled). 'The only metabolite identified in humans is the S-oxide' (FDA label). It is NOT modelled as a parent->metabolite pair because the label gives no fraction-formed - metabolic elimination is only 30-35% overall and the S-oxide's specific formation fraction is not a citable single number (the same reason the metronidazole hydroxy metabolite and procainamide->NAPA stay deferred). Noted for a possible future metabolite pair if a citable fm surfaces.

ROUTES. Oral (F ~0.43) and IV are both real marketed products (Pepcid tablets and Pepcid injection). The IV form is given by slow injection or infusion, so iv_infusion is the realistic IV route; iv_bolus is offered with F = 1 by definition as the instantaneous-input idealisation, and it makes the incomplete oral bioavailability visible (oral Cmax is ~43% of the IV level at equal dose).

EDUCATIONAL ILLUSTRATION ONLY - not for dosing.
